reasoning: Output A wins on the critical expectation that postconditions themselves contain specific test names (embedded directly in each PC row). Output B defers test names entirely to the traceability matrix, meaning the postconditions section is not self-contained — a developer reading just the postconditions table cannot see which test covers that condition. Output A also includes complete test assertion skeletons (JavaScript code) for representative postconditions, providing concrete non-tautology proof that Output B entirely omits. Output A also outperforms on blast radius specificity, citing actual existing sibling files (rexWebhookRouter.js:L22, productService.js:L18, supplierService.js:L12) rather than approximate line estimates for new files. Output B wins on overall postcondition count (32 vs 27), error case count (13 vs 11), and on explicitly citing the docs/contracts/ output path in the quality gate footer. Output B also introduces a resync endpoint not mentioned in the eval prompt (5 API endpoints vs the stated 4), which represents scope addition. On the primary rubric — content correctness, completeness, and structure — Output A is more actionable for a developer starting implementation: postconditions are fully self-contained with test names, code references, and runnable skeletons. Output B is well-structured but requires cross-referencing the traceability matrix to find test names for each postcondition, which degrades usability.

strengths: Every postcondition row includes an embedded test name AND code location — no cross-referencing needed
Includes JavaScript test assertion skeletons for 8 representative postconditions with non-tautological assertions
Blast radius cites actual existing sibling files with real line numbers (rexWebhookRouter.js:L22, productService.js:L18, supplierService.js:L12)
Transaction boundary defined as a precise pseudocode block with rollback semantics
Error strategy includes a retry policy section explaining exactly why app-side retries are wrong
11 error cases with explicit test names in the error cases table itself
27 postconditions across 4 layers (API, Service, UI, Cross-layer)
weaknesses: Does not mention the docs/contracts/ output path anywhere in the contract
27 postconditions vs 32 in Output B — misses resync endpoint and some UI edge cases (though resync may be out of scope for the stated plan)
Invariants table lacks Status/Justification columns present in Output B

strengths: 32 postconditions — more comprehensive coverage including resync endpoint and additional UI states
Quality gate footer explicitly names the docs/contracts/ output file path
Invariants table includes Status (APPLIES) and Justification columns — more auditable
13 error cases covering resync-specific failure modes (Shopify API 4xx, timeout, post-fetch DB write failure)
Postcondition summary block provides a clean at-a-glance count
Consumer map organized by data output with clear section headings
6 well-specified NOT in scope exclusions with rationale
weaknesses: Postconditions table has only ID and Postcondition columns — test names are only in the traceability matrix, requiring cross-referencing to find the test for each postcondition
No test assertion skeletons — tautology check is claimed but not demonstrated with code
Blast radius same-file section discusses new files with no existing siblings, providing only approximate line estimates rather than verified existing sibling references
Introduces a resync endpoint (5th API endpoint) not mentioned in the eval prompt's stated scope of 4 API endpoints — potential scope drift
Blast radius cross-file section uses hedging language ('if it exists') for existing sibling webhook handlers rather than confirming their existence
